question: I am not a registered reader of the Hong Kong Public Libraries (HKPL). Can I use the reference library services and collections?
expected_answer: Reference materials kept on shelves are accessible to all library users. For access to reference materials held in on-site reserve stacks, you have to enquire the counter staff.
agent_answer:
Reference materials kept on shelves are accessible to all library users, including those who are not registered readers. However, for access to reference materials held in on-site reserve stacks, you must enquire with the counter staff.

question: Why reference materials cannot be loaned?
expected_answer: Owing to the unique role and functions of reference libraries, reference materials may only be used inside the reference libraries, with the purpose of providing the general public with immediate access to up-to-date information during the library opening hours. To this end, an adequate reference collection needs to be in place. In addition, reference libraries serve to preserve publications which are essential for the study of local history and research into specific areas. Reference libraries also house rare books, flimsy items, expensive and valuable sets, and special reference collections deposited by other organisations, which are not suitable for circulation.
agent_answer:
Reference materials cannot be loaned because they must remain in the library to provide the public with immediate access to up-to-date information during opening hours and to preserve essential publications, rare books, and valuable sets for local history and research. Additionally, many of these items are not suitable for circulation due to their fragility or high value.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – reference_services: Why reference materials cannot be loaned?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

question: Given that reference materials cannot be loaned, can I photocopy them at the libraries?
expected_answer: Self-service photocopiers are available for public use in all reference libraries and district libraries. Any person who makes copies of any library material must comply with the Copyright Ordinance ( Cap 528 ). Generally speaking, unauthorised copying of copyright works is not allowed except for certain exceptions as stipulated in the Copyright Ordinance. Subject to conditions, fair dealing for research and private study, criticism, review and news reporting is permitted under the Ordinance. For details, please refer to the relevant provisions of the Copyright Ordinance. Nevertheless, library users should be cautious about the amount of copying made to avoid possible infringement of copyright. For example, copying a large portion of a book may constitute an infringement. An extract of the relevant parts of the Copyright Ordinance is posted in the vicinity of the photocopiers in all libraries. Extract of the Copyright Ordinance 23. Infringement of copyright by copying (1) The copying of the work is an act restricted by the copyright in every description of copyright work; and references in this Part to copying and copies are construed as follows. (2) Copying of a work means reproducing the work in any material form. This includes storing the work in any medium by electronic means. (3) In relation to an artistic work copying includes the making of a copy in 3 dimensions of a 2-dimensional work and the making of a copy in 2 dimensions of a 3-dimensional work. (4) Copying in relation to a film, television broadcast or cable programme includes making a photograph of the whole or any substantial part of any image forming part of the film, broadcast or cable programme. (5) Copying in relation to the typographical arrangement of a published edition means making a facsimile copy of the arrangement. (6) Copying in relation to any description of work includes the making of copies which are transient or are incidental to some other use of the work. [cf. 1988 c. 48 s. 17 U.K.] 38. Research and private study (1) Fair dealing with a work for the purposes of research or private study does not infringe any copyright in the work or, in the case of a published edition, in the typographical arrangement. (Amended 15 of 2007 s.12) (2) Copying by a person other than the researcher or student himself is not fair dealing if- (a) in the case of a librarian, or a person acting on behalf of a librarian, he does anything which regulations under section 49 would not permit to be done under section 47 or 48 (articles or parts of published works: restriction on multiple copies of same material); or (b) in any other case, the person doing the copying knows or has reason to believe that it will result in copies of substantially the same material being provided to more than one person at substantially the same time and for substantially the same purpose. (3) In determining whether any dealing with a work is fair dealing under subsection (1), the court shall take into account all the circumstances of the case and, in particular- (a) the purpose and nature of the dealing, including whether the dealing is for a non-profit-making purpose and whether the dealing is of a commercial nature; (b) the nature of the work; (c) the amount and substantiality of the portion dealt with in relation to the work as a whole; and (d) the effect of the dealing on the potential market for or value of the work.” (Replaced 15 of 2007 s.12) [cf. 1988 c. 48 s. 29 U.K.]
agent_answer:
Yes, self-service photocopiers are available for public use in all reference libraries and district libraries. However, you must comply with the Copyright Ordinance, and copying a large portion of a book may constitute an infringement of copyright.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – reference_services: Given that reference materials cannot be loaned, can I photocopy them at the libraries?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

question: Where can I find the Laws and Gazette of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (HKSAR)?
expected_answer: Full sets of the bilingual Laws of the HKSAR are kept in the Central Reference Library of the Hong Kong Central Library, and respective Reference Libraries of City Hall Public Library, Kowloon Public Library, Sha Tin Public Library, Tsuen Wan Public Library, Tuen Mun Public Library and Ping Shan Tin Shui Wai Public Library. These sets of laws are updated annually. The latest version of the Laws of the HKSAR is also accessible at Hong Kong e-Legislation on the homepage of the Department of Justice. Printed copies of the HKSAR Government Gazette are also available at the above reference libraries. The Central Reference Library has the continuous holding from 1853 to the current issue, while other reference libraries have varied holdings starting from the 1990s. You may contact individual reference libraries for details of their holdings. Previous issues of the HKSAR Government Gazette since 19 May 2000 are available for searching online. In addition, the Government Gazette from 1842 to 1941 is also available for searching and viewing at the Hong Kong Government Reports Online (1842 to 1941) on the website of The University of Hong Kong Libraries.
agent_answer:
Full sets of the bilingual Laws of the HKSAR are kept in the Central Reference Library of the Hong Kong Central Library and specific reference libraries including City Hall, Kowloon, Sha Tin, Tsuen Wan, Tuen Mun, and Ping Shan Tin Shui Wai Public Libraries. Printed copies of the HKSAR Government Gazette are also available at these locations, with the Central Reference Library holding issues from 1853 to the current issue. Additionally, previous issues of the Gazette since 19 May 2000 can be searched online, and historical issues from 1842 to 1941 are available via the Hong Kong Government Reports Online on the University of Hong Kong Libraries website.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – collections: Where can I find the Laws and Gazette of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (HKSAR)?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

question: How to access e-resources via the Internet?
expected_answer: Registered readers of the HKPL, i.e. library card holders or readers who have applied for using their Smart ID cards to use library services, can access e-resources of the HKPL via the Internet. To gain access to e-resources, you are required to login your library account by entering your library card number and password . If you have applied for using your smart ID card for library services, you may enter your HKID card number in the library account field. Please enter all letters and digits without the bracket, e.g. a1234567 for HKID card number A123456(7). The default password will be the last 4 digits of the reader’s registered phone number . For further enquiry on your password, please contact the duty staff of any public library during the opening hours .
agent_answer:
Registered readers can access e-resources via the Internet by logging into their library account using their library card number and password, or their HKID card number if they have applied for Smart ID card services. The default password is the last four digits of the reader's registered phone number.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – e_resources: How to access e-resources via the Internet?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

expected_answer: Registered readers of the HKPL can install mobile apps on their mobile devices (tablets or smart phones) to access the following e-resources. e-Resource Mobile App Smart Phone Tablet 1 Britannica Collective Britannica Collective for HKPL √ √ 2 eBooks on EBSCOhost Adobe Digital Editions √ √ 3 Flipster Flipster √ √ 4 FunPark FunParkç«¥æ¸å¤¢å·¥å» √ √ 5 HyRead ebook HyRead √ √ 6 Jin Yong Martial Arts Novels Audio Collection Nowbook Digital Library √ √ 7 Kono Libraries Kono Libraries √ √ 8 Naxos Music Library Naxos Music Library √ √ 9 Naxos Music Library Jazz Naxos Music Library Jazz √ √ 10 OverDrive Chinese eBooks Libby √ √ 11 OverDrive eBooks Libby √ √ 12 OverDrive Magazines Libby √ √ 13 ProQuest Ebook Central Adobe Digital Editions √ √ 14 SUEP "Hong Kong Renowned Writers" e-Book Nowbook Digital Library √ √ 15 SUEP e-Book Nowbook Digital Library √ √
agent_answer:
Registered HKPL readers can use mobile apps (for smartphones or tablets) to access the following e-resources: Britannica Collective, eBooks on EBSCOhost, Flipster, FunPark, HyRead ebook, Jin Yong Martial Arts Novels Audio Collection, Kono Libraries, Naxos Music Library (including Jazz), OverDrive (Chinese eBooks, eBooks, and Magazines via Libby), ProQuest Ebook Central, SUEP "Hong Kong Renowned Writers" e-Book, and SUEP e-Book.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – e_resources: Which e-resources have mobile apps?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

question: How can people with visual impairment gain access to e-books?
expected_answer: People with visual impairment can listen to audio e-books in “ Naxos Spoken Word Library ”, or gain access to all English e-books through the computers that are installed with the Cantonese and English voice synthesiser software in static libraries.
agent_answer:
People with visual impairment can listen to audio e-books via the "Naxos Spoken Word Library" or access all English e-books using computers in static libraries equipped with Cantonese and English voice synthesiser software.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – e_resources: How can people with visual impairment gain access to e-books?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

question: How can I seek help when I encounter problems in using e-resources?
expected_answer: If problems are encountered in using e-resources, please refer to the user guides on the webpage. You may also contact the duty librarian of the Reference and Information Enquiry Centre at 2921 0222 during the opening hours of the Hong Kong Central Library .
agent_answer:
If you encounter problems using e-resources, please refer to the user guides on the webpage or contact the duty librarian of the Reference and Information Enquiry Centre at 2921 0222 during the opening hours of the Hong Kong Central Library.
Sources:
- HKPL Ask a Librarian FAQ – e_resources: How can I seek help when I encounter problems in using e-resources?
  https://www.hkpl.gov.hk/en/ask-a-librarian/faq.html

question: Follow-up: Do I need it?
expected_answer: The agent should use session history to resolve the follow-up question.
agent_answer:
